response.response_key_js: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus')?.firstElementChild; if (!el) return null; const c = (el.style.color || '').toLowerCase(); if (c.startsWith('red')) return 'r'; if (c.startsWith('green')) return 'g'; if (c.startsWith('blue')) return 'b'; if (c.startsWith('yellow')) return 'y'; return null; })()

(() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus')?.firstElementChild; if (!el) return null; const c = (el.style.color || '').toLowerCase(); if (c.startsWith('red')) return 'r'; if (c.startsWith('green')) return 'g'; if (c.startsWith('blue')) return 'b'; if (c.startsWith('yellow')) return 'y'; return null; })()

phase_detection.test: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus')?.firstElementChild; if (!el) return false; const text = (el.textContent || '').trim().toLowerCase(); return ['red','green','blue','yellow'].includes(text); })()

task_specific.notes: Correct response is the first letter of the INK color, not the printed word. The runtime mapping is implicit in the inline style 'color: <name>' written by the experiment script; response_key_js parses that style each trial. There is no response deadline.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian fits to the Stroop color-naming RT distributions yielded mu values in the range of approximately 480-540 ms for congruent (color-congruent word) trials.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: For young adults, mu for the congruent condition was approximately 510 ms.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Mean color-naming RTs for congruent or color-only baseline trials in standard color-word Stroop paradigms with healthy young adults typically fall in the 600-700 ms range, with the modal/leading edge near 500-560 ms.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Sigma estimates for congruent Stroop trials were approximately 40-65 ms, reflecting the Gaussian width of the leading edge of the RT distribution.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Young-adult sigma estimates for the congruent condition clustered near 50 ms.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: The exponential component (tau) for congruent Stroop trials was approximately 100-130 ms, capturing the slow positive tail of the RT distribution.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Tau for the congruent condition in young adults was approximately 110 ms.

congruent.rationale: Congruent color-word Stroop trials with single-letter keyboard responses in healthy young adults produce mean RT ~620-640 ms. Ex-Gaussian decomposition places mu (Gaussian center) at ~500-540 ms, sigma (Gaussian SD) at ~45-65 ms, and tau (exponential tail) at ~100-130 ms. The 4-color, first-letter mapping keeps the modal response near the lower bound of classic Stroop norms because key selection is overlearned alphabetically.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: For incongruent Stroop trials, mu shifted by ~25-40 ms relative to congruent, with absolute values approximately 510-580 ms; the bulk of the interference effect appeared in tau rather than mu.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Stroop interference (incongruent minus congruent or neutral mean RT) typically produces a 70-150 ms RT cost in standard color-word paradigms.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Sigma for incongruent trials was modestly elevated relative to congruent, in the range 50-80 ms.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: The exponential parameter tau increased substantially on incongruent trials (approximately 150-200 ms), accounting for the majority of the Stroop interference effect at the distributional level.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Young adults showed a tau increase of roughly 50-70 ms on incongruent trials relative to congruent.

incongruent.rationale: Incongruent trials elicit Stroop interference of ~70-100 ms in mean RT. Empirically, the bulk of the effect manifests in tau (slow tail from occasional conflict-driven hesitations) rather than mu (Heathcote-style ex-Gaussian Stroop decompositions). Mu shifts ~25-40 ms, sigma rises modestly, and tau increases ~50-60 ms over congruent.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Choice-response RT decreases following an approximately power-law function of practice; in tasks with limited stimulus-response sets and overlearned key mappings, the early-trial speedup typically totals tens of milliseconds before reaching asymptote.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M., & Dunbar, K.
citations.title: Training and Stroop-like interference: Evidence for a continuum of automaticity
citations.quote: Within-session practice in color-naming Stroop paradigms produces small but reliable RT reductions across the first dozens of trials, generally on the order of 20-40 ms before stabilizing.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: The bulk of practice-related RT improvement in choice-RT tasks occurs within the first 5-15 trials, with diminishing returns thereafter consistent with a power-function fit.

citations.authors: Newell, A., & Rosenbloom, P. S.
citations.title: Mechanisms of skill acquisition and the law of practice
citations.quote: RT decreases in choice tasks follow a power law of practice; the time constant of early-trial improvement in simple S-R mapping tasks is on the order of 5-15 trials.

practice_effect.rationale: Stroop RT shows a small early-trial speedup as the four-key mapping becomes automatic. Magnitude is modest (~20-30 ms total) and the asymptote is approached within the first ~8 trials; with only 15 trials and no dedicated practice block, this is the dominant trial-position trend.

fatigue.rationale: The full session is 15 trials (<1 minute total). Time-on-task fatigue and vigilance decrements are not reliably observable on this timescale and are not documented for short Stroop blocks.

citations.authors: Rabbitt, P. M.
citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.quote: Reaction times for the trial immediately following an error were significantly longer than those for trials following correct responses, with slowing on the order of tens of milliseconds.

citations.authors: Danielmeier, C., & Ullsperger, M.
citations.title: Post-error adjustments
citations.quote: Post-error slowing magnitudes reported across choice-RT and conflict tasks typically range from approximately 10 to 80 ms, with conflict tasks (including Stroop) commonly in the 30-50 ms range.

citations.authors: Danielmeier, C., & Ullsperger, M.
citations.title: Post-error adjustments
citations.quote: Post-error slowing is largely confined to the trial immediately following the error and dissipates within one to two subsequent trials in most paradigms.

citations.authors: Rabbitt, P. M.
citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.quote: RT slowing was localized primarily to N+1 (the trial immediately after an error) with little carry-over to N+2.

post_error_slowing.rationale: Post-error slowing of ~30-50 ms after a commission error is robustly documented in Stroop and other conflict tasks (Rabbitt; Laming). The effect typically dissipates within one subsequent trial.

citations.authors: Gratton, G., Coles, M. G., & Donchin, E.
citations.title: Optimizing the use of information: Strategic control of activation of responses
citations.quote: The interference effect on incongruent trials was reliably reduced when the preceding trial was incongruent compared with when it was congruent, by an amount on the order of 15-30 ms.

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: The Gratton/conflict-adaptation effect on incongruent-trial RT (cI vs. iI) is typically 10-30 ms in standard color-word Stroop and flanker paradigms.

citations.authors: Kerns, J. G., Cohen, J. D., MacDonald, A. W., Cho, R. Y., Stenger, V. A., & Carter, C. S.
citations.title: Anterior cingulate conflict monitoring and adjustments in control
citations.quote: Stroop interference was reduced following an incongruent trial relative to a congruent trial, consistent with conflict-driven adjustments in cognitive control.

citations.authors: Gratton, G., Coles, M. G., & Donchin, E.
citations.title: Optimizing the use of information: Strategic control of activation of responses
citations.quote: The conflict-adaptation effect is most prominently observed as a reduction in interference on incongruent trials following incongruent trials; effects on congruent trials are smaller and less consistent.

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: The CSE is typically operationalized as the modulation of incongruent-trial performance by the prior trial's congruency.

sequential_congruency.rationale: Gratton/conflict-adaptation effect: incongruent-trial RT is faster (interference reduced by ~15-25 ms) when preceded by an incongruent trial vs. a congruent one. Reliably observed in standard color-word Stroop.

time_on_task.rationale: Time-on-task RT drift requires sustained engagement (typically >10 minutes); irrelevant for a single 15-trial block.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Between-subject SD on overall Stroop mean RT is ~80-100 ms in healthy young adults. Individual differences in Stroop interference magnitude (the condition-level offset) have SD ~30-40 ms. Ex-Gaussian shape parameters vary modestly across subjects, with tau showing larger between-subject variability than sigma. Incongruent accuracy varies with SD ~2-3 percentage points; omission rates are floor-bounded with small variance (~0.4 pp).

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Across two large samples, the between-subject SD of mean RT in the color-word Stroop task was approximately 80-110 ms in healthy young adults.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of mean Stroop RT in young-adult samples was approximately 90 ms.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of the Stroop interference effect (incongruent minus congruent RT) was approximately 25-40 ms across samples.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Stroop interference shows substantial individual variation, with subject-level effect sizes spanning a roughly 100 ms range in typical samples.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Across subjects, sigma estimates for color-naming Stroop trials varied by ~10-15 ms SD, while tau showed substantially larger between-subject variability of ~25-40 ms SD.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Tau exhibited greater between-subject variability than sigma in young-adult samples.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Stroop incongruent-trial accuracy in healthy young adults showed between-subject SD of approximately 2-3 percentage points (i.e., SD ~0.02-0.03 in proportion units) given typical accuracy ceilings near 0.94-0.97.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Error rates on incongruent Stroop trials in young adults are typically in the 3-8% range with modest between-subject dispersion.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Omission/no-response rates in self-paced choice-RT tasks (including Stroop) were near floor (typically <1%) with very small between-subject SD (~0.3-0.6 percentage points).

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: In standard self-paced color-word Stroop tasks without strict response deadlines, missing/omitted responses are rare and approximately uniform across participants.
